Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I had a chunk of free time, then put on lecture recordings and listen while doing other stuff around my place. Since I don't have a lot of resources, I couldn't always study in ideal conditions, so I made do with what I had. I'd say I studied in bursts rather than having a consistent daily schedule. Sometimes I'd cram 5-6 hours in one day, then nothing for a few days.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I wish I could say I was more consistent, but the reality is that when assignments were due, I'd suddenly become super focused. Other weeks when nothing was pressing, I'd slack off and barely touch the course materials. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but with everything going on, it's what worked for me at the time.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes boredom, ironically—I'd log in just to see if there was anything interesting happening in the discussion forums. The gamified tasks actually helped because earning points or badges gave me a small reason to check in more often, even when I wasn't feeling motivated.
Q4: There were a few weeks where logging in became more automatic, especially when the gamified elements were active. Seeing my progress on leaderboards or unlocking achievements made it feel less like a chore. But I wouldn't say it ever became a deeply ingrained habit—it was more like a temporary pattern that faded when the novelty wore off or when my anxiety about other things spiked.
Q5: The lecture recordings were my lifeline since I learn best by listening. I'd play them multiple times, sometimes while commuting or doing chores. The discussion forums were also surprisingly helpful—reading other students' questions and answers helped clarify concepts I was confused about. Those two things probably contributed most to my exam performance.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and PDFs. With limited data and resources, downloading heavy files wasn't always practical. Plus, reading dense text just doesn't stick for me the way hearing someone explain it does. I'd skim them occasionally but rarely gave them proper attention. The optional quizzes I also skipped unless they were tied to some gamified reward.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to exams or assignments. Also, if it was engaging—like the gamified tasks that made things feel less monotonous. When something felt like busywork or when I couldn't connect it to my grade, my motivation tanked. I needed to feel like my limited time and energy were being invested wisely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to prioritize. Required ones had this weight of consequence attached—if I didn't do them, my grade suffered. Optional stuff, even if interesting, got pushed aside because I was already anxious about keeping up with the mandatory work. The gamified optional tasks were an exception because they felt more like breaks than additional burden.
Q9: Absolutely. Once I downloaded lecture recordings, I'd listen to them offline repeatedly. I also took handwritten notes while listening because that helps me process information better. I used free YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was a huge part of how I actually learned the material.
Q10: Most of my real studying happened away from the LMS. I'd be on the bus with my earphones in, replaying a lecture for the third time, or sitting at home reviewing my handwritten notes. I also studied with a couple of classmates in person sometimes, quizzing each other verbally. The LMS would just show one download or one video view, but the actual learning was way more extensive.
Q11: Yeah, that was my standard approach. I'd access a lecture video once, download it or note down the key points, and then work with that material offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so downloading things and working offline was more practical. One login might represent hours of subsequent offline study.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety was really high. I'd feel overwhelmed and just... disengage. Sometimes I wouldn't log in for over a week because facing the course felt too stressful. Competing responsibilities at home also pulled my attention away. Those low points didn't last forever, but they definitely showed up in my attendance stats.
Q13: All of those factors played a role at different times. Fatigue was big—I'd be too mentally exhausted to engage meaningfully even when I logged in. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while. When responsibilities at home piled up, the LMS became the last thing on my mind. I had to pick my battles with limited energy.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything. Those sessions were more about going through the motions than actual learning. My anxiety made it hard to concentrate even when I was technically 'present' on the platform.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. The anxiety paradox—the more important something was, the more it stressed me out, so I'd avoid it. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, then tomorrow became next week. It wasn't laziness; it was more like paralysis. The gamified tasks helped break that cycle sometimes because they felt lower stakes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation system. Without them, I'd drift. As a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become hyper-focused and productive. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered in the 48 hours before something was due. It's not ideal, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my boredom and anxiety.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, made me feel like maybe I could actually do this. But if feedback was critical or if I got a lower score than expected, my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the course for a bit. I needed time to process criticism before I could use it constructively.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but the pacing sometimes felt off for me. When too many things were due in the same week, I'd get overwhelmed and my participation would drop. The gamified elements added some structure that actually helped—having small, achievable goals broke the monotony. But overall, I needed more flexibility than the course naturally provided.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they turned boring stuff into something slightly more interesting. What made it harder was when materials were only available in formats that didn't suit my learning style—like dense readings with no audio alternative. Also, unclear instructions on assignments increased my anxiety and made me procrastinate more.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or confused. They might be clicking around, rewatching things, trying to understand but not feeling confident enough to submit work. Or maybe they're perfectionists who keep revising but never feel ready to turn things in. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My exam score was high even though my attendance and LMS activity were mediocre. I did most of my effective studying offline—listening to recordings, reviewing notes, discussing with peers in person. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the course content. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you spent. That's useful for seeing patterns. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you were actually focused or just had the tab open. They miss offline studying entirely. They can't capture understanding, confusion, emotional state, or the real reasons behind someone's behavior.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when I'm genuinely curious, asking questions in my head, connecting ideas to things I already know. It's different from just completing tasks. Some of my most engaged moments happened offline, alone with my notes and thoughts, not on any platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout the course, or how that affected my ability to engage consistently. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures offline, or the conversations with classmates that helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show that despite feeling bored and anxious a lot, I genuinely wanted to learn and pushed through anyway.
